serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving is 2 hard-boiled eggs (approximately 100g), seasoned with a light sprinkle of salt, black pepper, and chilli powder.

nutrient_source.title: Eggs, grade A, large, egg whole, hard-boiled — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID: 173424)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy hard-boiled egg values used as the primary nutrient basis. The HPB Singapore input record reported energy 55 kcal/100g, protein 10.7g, fat 0.0g, and carbohydrate 2.36g; however, fat=0.0g for a whole hard-boiled egg is physiologically implausible (egg yolk contains approximately 9–10g fat/100g). USDA SR Legacy (FDC 173424) reports energy 155 kcal, protein 12.6g, fat 10.6g, carbohydrate 1.12g per 100g for hard-boiled egg, which is consistent with established food composition science. The USDA values are adopted. Seasoning additions (salt, pepper, chilli powder) contribute negligible macronutrients at typical sprinkle quantities and are not separately adjusted. Sodium is slightly elevated from added salt; a conservative estimate of 140mg/100g is used, consistent with HPB Singapore data for seasoned boiled eggs.

gi_source.title: Hard-boiled eggs — glycemic index not applicable due to negligible carbohydrate content
gi_source.source_match_notes: Hard-boiled eggs contain negligible carbohydrates (1.12g/100g); glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates. Per the Veritas q4 rule for low/zero-carbohydrate foods, glycemic_index is set to null (not 0), gi_category is set to not_applicable, and glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0. The University of Sydney GI Database does not list a GI value for hard-boiled eggs for this reason.

hypertension.key_factor: Low-to-moderate sodium with added seasoning salt
hypertension.guidance: Hard-boiled eggs are a minimally processed, low-sodium protein source; however, individuals with hypertension should limit or omit the added salt sprinkle and avoid pairing with high-sodium condiments such as soy sauce or ketchup to keep total sodium intake within the recommended 2,000mg/day limit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate content with high-quality protein
type2_diabetes.guidance: With only 1.12g carbohydrate per 100g and a high protein content of 12.6g, hard-boiled eggs have a negligible impact on postprandial blood glucose and are a suitable protein choice for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, though dietary cholesterol (373mg/100g) should be monitored in line with individual clinical guidance.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High dietary cholesterol (373mg/100g) with moderate saturated fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Eggs provide high-quality protein and beneficial micronutrients but contain 373mg cholesterol per 100g; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption to 1–2 eggs per day as part of a heart-healthy dietary pattern and avoid adding salt or high-fat condiments.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Arachidonic acid in egg yolk and high cholesterol
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Hard-boiled eggs provide vitamin D, calcium, and vitamin E relevant to bone protection in rheumatoid arthritis, but egg yolk arachidonic acid may contribute to omega-6 load; limit to 1–2 eggs per day and monitor lipid levels if on RA medications affecting lipid metabolism.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hard-Boiled Eggs with Salt, Pepper and Chilli Powder is a simple, protein-rich dish consisting of two whole chicken eggs that have been hard-boiled until the yolk is fully set, then lightly seasoned with a sprinkle of salt, ground black pepper, and chilli powder. The dish is classified as non-vegetarian due to the use of whole eggs including the yolk. The texture is firm throughout — the white is rubbery and dense while the yolk is dry and crumbly. The seasoning adds a mild heat and savoury note without significantly altering the macronutrient profile. Nutritionally, hard-boiled eggs are one of the most complete whole-food protein sources available, providing approximately 12.6g protein, 10.6g fat (of which 3.27g is saturated), and only 1.12g carbohydrate per 100g, yielding 155 kcal. They are rich in fat-soluble vitamins including vitamin A (149mcg RAE), vitamin D (2.0mcg), and vitamin E (1.03mg), as well as B-vitamins including riboflavin (0.51mg), B12 (1.11mcg), and folate (44mcg DFE). Cholesterol content is high at 373mg/100g, which is clinically relevant for cardiovascular and diabetic dietary planning. The glycemic index is not applicable due to negligible carbohydrate content. This dish is commonly consumed as a snack, breakfast item, or protein accompaniment in Singapore hawker culture and home cooking, and is suitable for low-carbohydrate, gluten-free, and halal dietary patterns.
common_user_queries: Are boiled eggs good for diabetes?
How many calories in 2 boiled eggs?
Is boiled egg good for high blood pressure?
Can I eat boiled eggs if I have high cholesterol?
Boiled egg with chilli powder calories

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC 173424), a primary approved source for hard-boiled eggs. The HPB input fat value of 0.0g was identified as physiologically implausible and corrected using USDA data. All mandatory multilingual names for a Singaporean dish are present. Ontology codes are defensible and verified.
notes: 1. The input record (HPB) reported fat_g = 0.0 for hard-boiled eggs. This is physiologically implausible for a whole egg containing yolk (egg yolk is approximately 30% fat by weight). USDA SR Legacy FDC 173424 reports fat_g = 10.6g/100g, which is adopted. The HPB fat value is considered a data entry error and is not used. 2. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0: confirmed zero — eggs contain no dietary fibre per USDA FoodData Central. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero — hard-boiled eggs contain no vitamin C per USDA FoodData Central. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. 3. Sodium of 140mg/100g is a conservative estimate accounting for the added salt sprinkle described in the dish; plain hard-boiled egg sodium per USDA is 124mg/100g. 4. Hard-boiled egg; glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (1.12g/100g). glycemic_index is set to null (not 0) and gi_category to not_applicable per Veritas q4 rule for low/zero-carbohydrate foods. 5. The dish name in the input contains a typographical error ('Bolied' instead of 'Boiled'); the canonical English name has been corrected to 'Hard-Boiled Eggs with Salt, Pepper and Chilli Powder'. The original misspelling is preserved as an alias for retrieval purposes. 6. Multilingual names provided for Singaporean cuisine category: English, Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi. 7. Aliases self-check: all strings in aliases_by_language (english, chinese, malay, tamil, hindi, romanized) verified to appear verbatim in the flat aliases array.
